data.title: Markets Are Souring On Trump's Iran Strategy
data.description:
Thesis Summary

We’re now into week three of the Iran war, and there still seems to be no end in sight.

What should have been a swift, surgical joint U.S.-Israeli bombing campaign against Iran has turned into something much bigger.

Iran is not Venezuela, with its 90 million inhabitants and strategic position and assets in the Middle East.

Today, the S&P 5000 finally broke below its 200 EMA, and as if Trump were tracking this, he quickly made two key announcements to lift stocks back up.

But the damage may be done, and even though Trump is trying to backtrack, Iran may not let him.

Let’s break down the latest in the Iran war below,

A War Built on Mixed Signals

Trump told CBS News on Monday that the war was "very complete, pretty much," claiming Iran's military capabilities had been wiped out.

But in my opinion, and I think one shared by markets, this doesn’t line up with much of the other news we’ve heard over the last week, which, if anything, points towards escalation.

We're now three weeks into this war, and Iran doesn't seem to want out. Tehran has retaliated with increased attacks on Israeli and U.S. assets across the Gulf region.

It’s no longer just oil ships now being able to move, but actual production facilities, like Qatar’s LNG, which are being hit.

If the war’s almost over, as Trump says, no one has told Iran.

Iran's foreign minister told PBS NewsHour that negotiating with the United States would not "be on the table."

Then Trump Backed Down

During these last three weeks, the market could be described as volatile but definitely in a downtrend.

Down over 6% since the all-time highs, the S&P 500 actually fell below its 200-week EMA today.

But as I write, the indexes are up 1%. This could be a technical bounce, but it’s likely driven by some key developments.

Recent news suggests that the U.S. may actually lift sanctions on Iranian oil. This could help alleviate global demand, but it seems like a Band-Aid on a much larger problem.

This Is Not Bullish

While markets are cheering for now, this is not something that seems bullish in the long term.

It’s a clear show that the U.S. has bitten off more than it can chew.

The operation is dragging on longer than expected, and it seems like a recession is a statistical likelihood now.

In all instances where oil has risen to these levels, a recession has followed.

And while the U.S. struggles to contain the situation in the Middle East, the real issue continues to escalate.

Meanwhile, China Circles…

As I’ve written about before, this is all an indirect attack on China. Both Iran and Venezuela were allies and energy suppliers to the country.

As a response, the situation with Taiwan is heating up, and the U.S. has even come out and stated it does not see China invading Taiwan as likely in 2027.

The fact that you have to come out and say this, to me, is not a very bullish sign.

Markets Don’t Trust Trump

The larger issue at hand here is that Trump has lost a lot of credibility in the last few weeks. His portrayal of the war has been rightfully met with skepticism.

And on many other fronts, he is also alienating the U.S. population.

Historically, this doesn’t bode well for markets.

Yes, in the long term, the market balances out and tends to shrug off political turmoil, but there have been so many instances where we can directly link market turmoil to the actions of the government.

The Great Depression followed the Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act, which devastated global commerce and popped the 1920s stock market bubble.

In 1971, we had the Nixon shock, which led to the closing of the gold window and at least contributed towards the following decade of stagflation.

And in 1979, Jimmy Carter had the "Crisis of Confidence”.

An energy crisis ended a period of growth and led to an increase in both inflation and interest rates as the economy struggled.

Eventually, Carter fired his entire cabinet, which fueled the perception of an erratic presidency without direction.

Could something similar happen today? It certainly doesn’t seem implausible.

What Should Investors Do?

The current macroeconomic climate is not favorable to stocks.

The Fed came out rather hawkish. We have an ongoing war and the added volatility of this being a midterm year.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has not moved in six months, and over the last month, every rally has been sold

This is not the time to take risks.

Discipline is important, and while I do think we will eventually see a return to growth, especially as AI productivity kicks in, the next 2-3 months could see markets retest important support levels.

Final Thoughts

Markets listen to Trump, and headlines matter. For a while, the President had good control over the narrative, but this is beginning to change.

The market is losing trust, and this creates uncertainty over the future, which is not good for returns.

The next few months will be challenging and, arguably, a make-or-break point that will define what the rest of this presidency looks like politically and economically.

data.title: ITWO: Russell 2000 Covered Call Strategy That Outperforms Its Peers
data.description:
The ProShares Russell 2000 High Income ETF (ITWO) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with direct exposure to the Russell 2000 Index while earning income through the use of zero days to expiration [0DTE] covered call options. The strategy may be exceptionally appealing to investors that want to gain exposure to the small-cap Index while earning returns in the form of income rather than purely price returns. Given the fund’s appealing performance relative to the underlying Index and the potential for a continued rotation into small-cap stocks, I believe ITWO can present an appealing case as an investment candidate; I am recommending ITWO with a Buy rating.

Investment Thesis for ITWO

ITWO provides investors with long exposure to the Russell 2000 Index while selling out-of-the-money 0DTE covered calls to provide both upside exposure and income. While covered call strategies typically limit upside exposure, ITWO’s use of 0DTE options minimize theta risk, or the time value of the options, narrowing the window for options to be exercised if the price of the underlying Index were to appreciate above the strike price of the options. Comparing 1-year performance to peer strategies, ITWO has largely outperformed the Russell 2000 synthetic covered call strategy managed by Defiance ETFs (IWMY) and the Global X Russell 2000 Covered Call ETF (RYLD), which invests in its own Russell 2000 Index fund (RSSL).

I believe the driving factor for ITWO's performance is that the strategy directly invests in the individual equities tied to the Index, potentially reducing NAV erosion and volatility risk. With respect to the underlying Index, ITWO's out-of-the-money 0DTE covered call strategy provides room for the Index to appreciate before risking a loss on the covered calls. With limited theta risk, this may provide the ETF with a strong buffer to ensure the equity holdings can realize upside potential.

Thematically, I have reason to believe that small-cap stocks will outperform large-cap market indices in 2026 for a variety of reasons, including the macroeconomic cycle, asset diversification, and microeconomic growth factors.

At the macroeconomic level, small-caps tend to outperform during periods of interest rate easing as set by the Federal Reserve. While the Fed left rates unchanged on March 18, 2026, a single, 25bps cut is expected for the remainder of 2026. Accordingly, rate policy is expected to be influenced by the rate of inflation, which is projected to be higher following the supply chain disruption in the Middle East that sent the price of oil above $100/bbl WTI before settling in the high $90/bbl range. With uncertainty as to the duration of the Iranian war, oil and natural gas prices could be a bellwether for higher inflation for 2026. If prices were to remain elevated, assuming the economy doesn’t undergo a correction, I believe a single rate cut may be in line. If the economy were to face further disruption, additional rate cuts may be necessary to stabilize employment and prices. Overall, I expect that oil prices will normalize throughout the duration of 2026 while remaining elevated above pre-war prices (high-$50 to low-$70/bbl), easing pressure on the headline inflation rate while creating an appealing market environment for constituents in the portfolio.

Interest rate cuts can benefit the financials sector, which makes up 18.33% of net assets in ITWO. Rate cuts may have the potential to bolster net interest margins, or the difference between borrowing and lending, as well as lowering the cost of borrowings which may entice companies to take on more debt. That being said, private lending may be entering a challenging market environment with the emergence of investors’ concerns regarding private credit funds. As a result of capital market dynamics, I believe smaller companies tend to utilize term loans and revolving credit facilities over public debt issuances as a way to finance projects, working capital, and bolt-on M&A deals. If the banks’ ability to offload risk to private credit funds were to become less available, the market could face significant risk of dried-up liquidity that may disrupt small-cap companies’ ability to borrow.

From an asset diversification standpoint, I have reason to believe investors are seeking to diversify away from mega-cap technology stocks, favoring small-cap stocks for diversification and expected performance. Taking into consideration sector concentration in the strategy, financials, health care, and industrials are the top 3 sectors in the Russell 2000 Index, with the information technology and communication services sectors making up roughly 15% of the Index. By comparison, these two sectors make up 43% of the S&P 500 Index (SPY) and 67% of the Nasdaq-100 Index (QQQ). For diversification, the Russell 2000 Index provides an appealing diversification factor that can provide investors with greater exposure to the industrials sector, which I believe can realize growth in 2026 driven by investments in engineering & construction services, aerospace & defense [including satellite communications], and advancements and adoption in AI that may help bolster margins.

For engineering & construction, the fund holds companies like Sterling Infrastructure, Inc. (STRL), Primoris Services Corporation (PRIM), and Argan, Inc. (AGX), amongst others, which may benefit from the large investment outlays for large-scale data centers and power infrastructure. As for aerospace & defense, the strategy provides exposure to growth companies like Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. (KTOS), AeroVironment, Inc. (AVAV), and Moog Inc. (MOG.A), amongst others, that may benefit from the increasing investments in munitions and defense modernization by the Department of War.

A defining factor is that companies in the industrials sector may be the primary target for AI adoption and industrial automation, utilizing robotics and automated process systems to enhance operations that may, in turn, reduce overall operating expenses. While I believe this theme is in the early adoption phase, I believe it can provide long-term value as these systems are further adopted.

While I believe there remains upside potential in the Index, the market has been faced with challenges since the start of 2026 with the emerging Iranian war and the possibility of a higher rate of inflation. These two factors could potentially negatively weigh on small-cap companies that may be more sensitive to incremental changes in prices. That being said, I believe that the oil market will normalize in due time as action is being taken to alleviate the supply constraints, including the enactment of the Defense Production Act that may effectively allow Sable Offshore Corp. (SOC) to restart production, a universal agreement across 32 member countries to release 400 million barrels of oil from their strategic petroleum reserves, and the “strategic waiver” for countries to import Russian oil “stranded at sea.” While the last two factors are temporary actions, the drive for increasing domestic production can potentially help normalize prices over time.

About ProShares Russell 2000 High Income ETF

ITWO was launched on September 4, 2024 on the Cboe BZX Exchange. The fund has a relatively low expense ratio of 55bps, an appealing feature when compared to peer covered call strategies. ITWO exhibits limited liquidity with $150 million in net assets with an average of $1.63 million in share value changing hands on a daily basis.

ITWO was designed to track the Cboe Russell 2000 Daily Covered Call index, which consists of long exposure to the Russell 2000 while gaining short exposure through the sale of the out-of-the-money [0DTE] Russell 2000 Index call options. ITWO takes a direct investment approach in managing the portfolio, investing in the individual constituents of the Index while simultaneously selling Index call options.

ITWO pays out a monthly distribution annualized at $4.64/share over the last twelve months, yielding 11.58%. Investors should consider that 95% of the most recent distribution was derived from return of capital. ROC is a tax-deferred benefit that reduces the cost basis of the investment over time. If the cost basis reaches $0/share, additional ROC will be taxed as capital gains. Distributions have varied from period to period, likely the result of premium earned from options sold. Options tend to yield a higher premium during periods of greater market volatility.

Risks Related to ITWO

ITWO employs a 0DTE covered call strategy tied to a portfolio of Russell 2000 constituents in order to provide investors with capital growth and income, potentially presenting certain risks that should be considered prior to making a final investment decision. 0DTE options are dependent on an active market and various other factors like volatility for price discovery of the contracts; tighter liquidity and lower volatility may result in less premium earned from the sale of call options. The covered call strategy may cap growth depending on the underlying market dynamics, though the fund presents some protection from theta and smaller movements through the sale of out-of-the-money 0DTE options. The strategy may exhibit some NAV decay over time, impacting the investor’s total returns. Distribution variability may create some risks for those utilizing the strategy to augment income.

Final Thoughts

ITWO can be an appealing investment strategy for those seeking to enhance their exposure to small-cap stocks, earning monthly income through a 0DTE covered call strategy.

data.title: Super Micro: Third Time Is Not The Charm (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Super Micro (SMCI) develops and markets server and storage solutions, currently down 27% around the open following charges on employee smuggling Nvidia AI chips into China. SMCI's stock has already been depressed with contracting margins, as the server market is extremely competitive with massive players like Dell, but one of the main issues of SMCI has started to show signs again despite solid sales in Q2 FY26, which is extreme mistrust of SMCI's management and its previous history. Despite my argument in my last article where margins have to improve, and the market is mispricing the server market, but that is now all out the window since the new allegations are of extreme measure, causing fears and doubts with countless questions from the market that will likely surround SMCI at least until the next earnings call which will completely kill the narrative on SMCI for a while that 2026 is their recovery year where sales will accelerate, which it has already proven in Q2 FY26. Hence, I'm downgrading SMCI to a 'Sell', as I gave it the benefit of the doubt when Ernst & Young unexpectedly resigned, which led to delay in reports but no wrongdoing was found, but constant distrust with the board just isn't a good look for investors looking at any company.

Q2 FY26 and Looking Forward

Before I give my thoughts on the current situation, I will give my thoughts on the quarter and the rest of FY26. This quarter was the breaking point for SMCI, since Q4 guided for $6.5B in sales for Q1 FY26, which was met with a pre release before Q1 that sales would be significantly less, as SMCI posted $5B in revenue, significantly missing sales as the stock took a massive hit. The massive negative reaction was also led by the fact that Q4 guided for $33B in revenue for FY26, with a consensus of $29.79B and 50% revenue growth YoY from FY25. This quarter exactly put SMCI back on its guidance, with $12.68B, which exceeded expectations massively.

This is further met with even higher guidance than their Q4 guidance, guiding for $40B in sales for the rest of FY26. This suggests 81.9% revenue growth YoY, which is extremely impressive for a company with a market cap of $13.5B at the time of writing.

The narrative remained the same for investors, as margins continue to contract as the competition remains extremely high with firms like Dell and HPE, which also have contracting margins as a result of aggressive price competition. As I also mentioned in my Dell article, Dell despite being the leader in servers, barely have 10% of unit share for x86 servers and 25% in mainstream shares, as no single firm dominates in the server market.

From my last analysis, it was noted by management that they are optimistic with their gross margins in Q4, which fell from 11.9% 1 year ago to 6.4% in Q2 FY26, as margins have yet to improve and will likely be the case looking forward.

From the earnings call:

We expect gross margins to be up 30 basis points relative to Q2 FY '26 levels.

It was noted that gross margins would improve, but by only 30 basis points, which suggests that gross margin will be 6.7%, which is still a contraction of 300 basis points with gross margin in Q3 FY25 at 9.7%. I believe their margins are still the biggest point to look out for in the next quarter, as the constant contraction of margins is one of investors biggest negative narratives on SMCI right now, as guiding for sequential expansion in margin in Q4 will be bullish for SMCI to finally flip their margins from contraction to expansion, as the adjusted EBITDA for 1H of FY26 actually fell as seen above, despite Q2 FY26 revenue growing 123%.

SMCI's History

In 2020, the SEC charged SMCI and the former CFO due to 'improperly and prematurely recognized revenue, including by recognizing revenue on goods sent to warehouses but not yet delivered to customers, shipping goods to customers prior to customer authorization, and shipping misassembled goods to customers.' This was the first occurrence where SMCI had permanently damaged investors trust.

The second time occurred when SMCI delayed its annual report in late August of 2024, needing to assess its internal controls, as Ernst & Young resigned as auditor in October, which truly affected investors since EY mentioned 'no longer be able to rely on management's and the Audit Committee's representations and to be unwilling to be associated with the financial statements prepared by management', as past fears starts to creep back in for potential accounting issues, which also led to delaying their September earnings report, as SMCI was risking delisting at this point. Though it was found that they had no wrongdoing after all, the damage had already been done. Delaying an annual report, quarterly reports as well as risking delisting is the last thing an investor wants; it kills investor confidence and transparency, which is expected to be perfected after the initial occurrence, but this continues to build up distrust between the board and investors.

The third occurrence is the newest one that just came to light, and again continues to create mistrust with investors and the board but I think that this occurrence is slightly different. From SMCI's statement, SMCI was not named as a defendant, but the VP of business development and a member of the board, and a sales manager have been placed under administrative leave, and a contractor has been immediately terminated, charged with conspiring to violate U.S. export-control laws.

The main problem this time is far different from the other 2 instances, propping up your balance sheet doesn't affect customers in the ecosystem, it hurts investors mainly rather than the business itself. Nvidia has worked closely with the government to reopen its China market again, making a chip to fill their China market alongside following regulations since they don't want China to gain a lead in the AI race, from back and forth of taking a cut of the sales and getting banned from China completely, is clear that Nvidia's most advanced tech has been banned from entering China, as smuggling is the only way that China receives the most advanced Nvidia chip in the market. This makes it seem that SMCI has undermined its relationship Nvidia, as SMCI's reputation of being trustworthy is on the line.

SMCI has deep ties to Nvidia, and this is directly undermining their relationships, which I think is extremely justified with Nvidia calling SMCI to follow strict compliance, as I believe that reputation and relationship are on the line for SMCI, as this is one of the last things Nvidia wants, a close vendor to have a board member allegedly smuggling into China, as this could further complex Nvidia's China business which is complex already over the last few months. This also means that SMCI's whole business and guidance is now a question mark for investors, as fear and doubts on serious counts will surround SMCI for the third time, which is an investor's worst nightmare. Across all these instances, SMCI still has the same CEO, and honestly at this point I think SMCI will likely be better off with a completely new management team, as I believe the distrust with management and investors will only lead to SMCI continuing to selloff.

Valuation

SMCI continues to trade at low premiums, as these are the premiums for March 19 closing, which already suggest undervaluation across the board compared to the sector median and its 5Y average, as its valuations do seem extremely cheap right now. With the current market cap around $13.5B, if SMCI does achieve its guidance of $40B, suggest that its P/S TTM would be 0.35, as SMCI is entering deep value territory.

I don't think that the current issue is valuation, as I believe the narrative on whether SMCI will potentially lose business is the bigger problem, as reputation and relationships are currently on the line as remember, SMCI doesn't really have a moat in the server market, as many different companies are involved in the market, so it wouldn't be a huge loss if Nvidia simply just increase its trust and reliance on a competitor like Dell, which it already has a long standing relationship with.

Final Thoughts

With SMCI not actually being in the indictment itself, I think the damage has already been done, and it isn't just some member of staff, but a member of the board of directors and co founder which also owns nearly $500M worth of stock before the dip, as I believe that relationship and reputation have been heavily affected with SMCI and its customers. Due to this, I believe that this creates fears and doubts on SMCI since its impressive guidance (sales not margin) is now a question mark, as investors have to go through another heavy round of fears and doubts with either a lack of transparency or simply a high executive not following regulation, and in this case I believe it is way more serious than previous occasions as if the Nvidia relationship is ruined, SMCI's downtrend might be just the start. I still have a small position and will be looking to close out the position as I have lost complete trust in SMCI.

data.title: Gold And Silver Are Still - 'My Precious'
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

After rising 61% and 139% YoY, respectively, in 2025, hugely outperforming the 16% gain for the S&P 500 Index (SPY), both Gold (GLD) and Silver (SLV), the two primary precious metals, declined sharply yesterday, with their YTD gains now at 7.6% and 1.96%, respectively.

Note that at the peak in January, both Gold and Silver were up 30%+ and 68%+ in 2026, as they surged to historic highs driven by a perfect storm of geopolitical uncertainty, a weakening dollar, central banks building reserves at a record pace, and export controls on Silver introduced by China that significantly tightened global supply.

However, the macroeconomic dynamics have fundamentally shifted since then, as both precious metals broke below their 100DMA (day moving average) yesterday before closing at or slightly higher than the key level, signaling reinforcing bearish technical momentum and continued downside pressure in the near term.

This is the case as spiking energy prices stemming from the Middle East war sparked market concerns over inflation, resulting in the Fed holding their interest rates steady at 3.5%-3.75%, instead of easing as was earlier expected from softening labor market conditions.

With real interest rates now slowly rising higher since March along with the U.S. dollar (DXY) strengthening, precious metals face increasing headwinds, along with the “stagflationary” component weighing down on Silver given its industrial sensitivity that is tied to economic activity.

Up until now, I have never written about Gold and Silver on the SA platform. However, up until February 2026, I held between 10 and 12% of my portfolio in both precious metals combined. In February 2026, I exited my Silver position, locking in a gain of close to 200%, while I have also been heavily trimming down my Gold position, the most recent one being on 03/18, locking in a gain of 100%+ across all the tranches.

In this post, I will explain the macroeconomic signals that I am watching to determine where and when I may re-enter the precious metals trade again, as I am long-term bullish on both of them.

A Brief Overview of the Macroeconomic Environment in the U.S.

Since the U.S.-Iran war began with direct military strikes on Iranian soil, oil prices have spiked higher, as measured by both Brent Crude (CO1:COM) and WTI (CL1:COM) rising over 40% in the month of March to approximately $100 a barrel.

The reason why it is a big deal is that Oil is one of those commodities that touches nearly every part of our lives, from the gasoline we pump into our cars to the cost of groceries and manufactured goods.

As Goldman Sachs analysts pointed out, every $10 increase in the price of oil could lead to inflation jumping by 0.3%, which could severely dampen the purchasing power of the household budget. This in turn increases the risk of an economic slowdown (or worse, recession), especially as consumer spending makes up for more than two-thirds of U.S. GDP.

Based on the latest inflation readings, we can clearly see pressures starting to build on PPI (producer price index), with the headline figure surging 0.7% for the month of February, more than double the 0.3% analysts were expecting, and the annual figure jumping to 3.4% (up from 2.9% in January), indicating that inflation is already percolating at the wholesale level before reaching consumers.

In all of this, what really complicated the Fed’s job even more was last month’s labor report, which saw the U.S. economy unexpectedly losing 92,000 jobs in February, far below analysts’ forecasts of a 59,000 gain, with the unemployment rate ticking up to 4.4%, while wage growth remained sticky.

This Is How The Fed Has Responded (So Far)

In response to the above, the Fed has decided to keep rates steady at 3.5%-3.75%, with Fed Chair Jerome Powell emphasizing that they are in a difficult situation, balancing a weakening labor market against new inflationary shocks from surging oil prices.

What is also important to note is that their Core and Headline PCE inflation expectations for 2026 are higher at 2.7%, up from their 2.4-2.5% projection back in December, while revising their GDP projections for 2026 slightly higher to 2.4% (up from 2.3% in their December projection).

In other words, while many are fearing the U.S. economy slipping into a stagflationary environment of low growth and high inflation, driven by the high oil prices, the Fed’s projections suggest otherwise.

It’s All About The Trajectory Of Real Interest Rates

Tying the current macroeconomic environment to the price action of Gold and Silver, it is primarily the rise in real interest rates since March that has contributed to the downward volatility.

You see, a real interest rate is the interest rate you get after stripping away the effect of inflation. Or, in other words, Real Interest Rate equals Nominal Interest Rate minus Inflation Rate.

In this case, the U.S. 10Y nominal bond yield (US10Y) has been on the rise since March, moving up from 3.96% to 4.28% as a result of the escalating Middle East conflict fueling energy prices and renewed inflation concerns. Similarly, the 10Y breakeven inflation rate has also moved up from 2.25% to 2.37% during this period of time, resulting in the 10Y TIPS (measure of real interest rates) moving up from 1.70% to 1.85%.

At the same time, the U.S. dollar has also strengthened as a result, and since gold and silver are priced in dollars, a stronger currency makes them more expensive for international buyers, further dampening demand for the precious metals.

From here onwards, these are the following scenarios that I will be monitoring to determine the path forward for the precious metals.

  1. Higher For Longer (Bad for Gold + Silver): This is where we are currently. In this case, both the 10Y and the 10Y breakeven rise on higher inflation expectations, with the nominal rates rising at a faster rate than the breakeven. In this case, real interest rates will continue to remain elevated at current levels or move up higher, especially if the inflation expectations worsen from here. In this scenario, I expect both Gold and Silver to remain under pressure and underperform the S&P 500.

  2. Slowdown/Recession (Good for Gold, Bad for Silver): By keeping interest rates elevated at 3.5-3.75%, the Fed essentially wants to anchor inflation expectations at current levels. Over a longer period of time, the restrictive monetary policy will eventually lead to inflation expectations coming down from the economy slowing down. This usually follows the Fed quickly easing monetary policy to stimulate the economy and avoid a deep recession. In this case, the 10Y nominal yield falls drastically, and so does breakeven, causing real interest rates to contract, which is usually a bullish sign for Gold. Silver on the other hand, may underperform in this scenario, as its performance is tied to improving economic activity.

  3. Stagflation (Good for Gold, Uncertain for Silver): In this case, we can expect inflation expectations to outpace nominal 10Y interest rates, narrowing the real interest rate. This is usually the case, as policymakers are often reluctant to raise nominal rates during supply shocks like the one we are facing today with oil rising above $100/barrel, as such an action can often trigger a recession. This environment too is conducive for Gold, as it acts as a “safe haven” when fiat currencies, in this case the U.S. dollar, lose purchasing power. The price performance of Silver is hard to predict in a stagflationary economy.

  4. Economic Reacceleration (Good for Gold, Great for Silver): In this scenario, we can expect inflation expectations to go down from easing supply shocks (de-escalation), thus leading to improved consumer sentiment and GDP growth. With lowering inflation expectations, the Fed too usually eases monetary policy, leading to real interest rates declining lower and holding steady afterward. While both Gold and Silver would benefit in this scenario, Silver would clearly outperform given its sensitivity to economic cycles.

Given that we are in Scenario 1, we can expect both Gold and Silver to continue facing pressure at current levels, at least until the supply-driven higher inflation expectations don’t peak out. I do not believe we will be headed towards the Stagflationary environment (Scenario 3) as a base case, but I believe Scenario 2, which is an economic slowdown, to be the most likely scenario next.

Plus, when we layer in the long-term U.S. government budget deficit projections of $1.9T, the long-term thesis behind the precious metals remains intact. This is the case, as the U.S. must issue more Treasury securities when it runs large deficits. This increased supply of debt often requires higher yields to attract enough buyers, which in turn pushes up the real interest rates. But, with interest payments/debt servicing costs simultaneously ballooning higher, the Fed will be forced to artificially keep interest rates low, risking fueling further inflation. In other words, with the Fed caught between managing its debt vs. fighting inflation, investors will naturally divert their capital towards gold and silver as hedges against the devaluation of the U.S. dollar.

We have seen central banks building up their gold reserves, with gold overtaking U.S. Treasuries as the largest reserve asset held by foreign central banks for the first time in 30 years in early 2026 to reduce their reliance on the dollar, which is also a tailwind for the precious metals, particularly Gold.

Assessing Potential Entry Points for the Precious Metals

With both Gold and Silver under pressure from the rising dollar and real yields, this is what TD Securities analyst Daniel Ghali said:

"Gold is now a very widely held position for institutional investors, ⁠and that has been on the back of the debasement trade over the last year. But the foundations of that trade are now weakening. For the near term, we continue to see risk to the downside. There is a very substantial amount of room for gold to sell off while maintaining its bull market era trend support."

Plus, when we look at the volume and open interest ratio in both Gold and Silver, we can see that it is trending higher, which means that the bears are gaining control in the short term under Scenario 1 that I have outlined in the previous section.

Therefore, until I see the volume and open interest ratio start to peak for both metals, I will continue to remain on the sidelines. However, I’m eyeing a potential entry back at $374 for Gold GLD, which will equate to $4151/ounce and also coincides with the 200DMA. For Silver, I am a bit more cautious in the short term, especially if the U.S. economy continues towards a slowdown.

My Final Verdict & Conclusions

While I have booked profits on both Gold and Silver and reduced my position size as the precious metals face headwinds from a strengthening dollar and rising real yields, I continue to remain long-term bullish on both, particularly Gold, as it has a higher chance of outperforming across the scenarios I outlined above. At the moment, I will be monitoring the trend of inflation expectations and the real yields on the fundamental side as well as the volume and open interest ratio on the technical side to start increasing my position back in Gold, while remaining a bit more cautious on Silver in the near term.

data.title: Viatris: Is It A Credible Turnaround Or Overly Optimistic 2030 Plan?
data.description:
Thesis: Aggressive long-term targets

As you know, Viatris Inc. (VTRS) has just outlined a pretty ambitious long-term strategy through to 2030. The aim is to transform its base business into a much more durable, higher-margin portfolio whilst still maintaining financial discipline. So the projection is for total revenue CAGR of 5% to 6%, adjusted EBITDA CAGR of 7% to 8%, and adjusted EPS CAGR of 9% to 10% through 2030. We can also include the potential additional drivers such as accretive business development and the launch of some of the pipeline candidates, selatogrel and cenerimod. This would make up the combined long-term targets. However, these are some rather tight long-term case targets, with the focus more on existing operations and near-term launches. For example, fast-acting meloxicam, Pitolisant and Effexor for GAD in Japan are expected to bring a wider portfolio mix by 2030. They're set to support the base business, which has an anticipated 3% to 4% revenue CAGR, 4% to 5% adjusted EBITDA CAGR, and 6% to 7% adjusted EPS CAGR. This would also be supported by about $650 million in gross cost savings, which the company identified in a recent enterprise-wide strategic review.

I think the important thing here is that the company expects decent cash flow generation, with over $2.7 billion in annual free cash flow under that base case and over $11 billion available for deployment by 2030. So in theory, that should allow both shareholder returns and disciplined growth initiatives. Management also mentioned that these targets should come with operational stability, portfolio evolution, near-term launches, and financial flexibility. So it seems the positioning here is for Viatris to sustain revenue and earnings growth over the decade whilst continuing to create value for patients/shareholders. As for targets, this all sounds rather good; however, the upside here hinges on a lot going right for Viatris. There is also a lot of execution risk to consider, as I'll explain.

FY25 overview

Just to go over the latest earnings at the end of last month, I thought Viatris delivered a pretty mixed set of results. For 4Q25, revenue jumped about 5% year-over-year to $3.7 billion, which was mainly driven by strong performance in their branded products across Developed Markets, Emerging Markets, and Greater China. Adjusted profitability also improved; we saw adjusted EBITDA hit $1.0 billion and adjusted EPS increase about 6% to $0.57. Cash generation was also quite strong, with operating cash flow up about 69% and free cash flow, after we exclude transaction costs, managed to reach $730 million. So overall, there were healthy underlying operations even though there were a number of challenges starting to impact the numbers, such as generic competition and pricing pressure in certain regions. There were also some hefty costs from restructuring and manufacturing issues.

For FY25, however, the headline numbers were definitely weighed down by structural and accounting factors, which isn’t what the market wanted to see. The stock was actually trading near a 52-week high in the mid-$16 range coming into earnings. But since then, the shares have pulled back about 16%. Total revenue for the year declined 3% to about $14.3 billion, which we can mainly put down to weakness in generics, but also some declines in Developed Markets and the JANZ region. The good news was that this was partially offset by a strong 8% growth figure in Greater China.

Overall, it was a net loss of $3.5 billion, of which we can put $2.9 billion down to a hefty goodwill impairment charge. So this was an accounting adjustment rather than a cash outflow, but still very significant. On an adjusted basis, it left net earnings at $2.77 billion, down almost 13% year-over-year, which goes to show there's some pressure on core profitability. As for guidance, management mentioned that the company is undergoing a big strategic transformation with the expectation of up to $650 million in cost savings. So the 2026 guidance suggests some stabilization, with expected revenue around $14.7 billion and adjusted EPS near the $2.40 range. We can also expect some new product launches.

Path to 2030

If you think about it, the path that Viatris management just laid out to hit those 2030 targets is actually pretty structured. From what I can see, it comes down to three core growth engines plus one financial lever that needs to work in their favor. First, the main case is for base business stabilization, and the mix shift would be the foundation. Here, management is expecting 3% to 4% revenue growth simply from improving its existing portfolio by shifting toward higher-margin generics, value-added medicines, and established brands, whilst still maintaining global scale. For example, they have some very established products like Effexor that are being expanded into new indications/geographies, with the most notable being generalized anxiety disorder in Japan. Keep in mind that this currently has limited approved treatment options, so it would create a pretty clear commercial opportunity, but also with limited competition, which is key. Elsewhere, we’re seeing that Viatris is introducing some value-added reformulations. The fast-acting version of meloxicam for acute pain is a big one. It recently demonstrated strong Phase 3 efficacy, with the design being to improve the onset time versus the standard NSAIDs. The goal here is that the onset time can offer that differentiated advantage and basically extract more value from an existing molecule rather than relying on entirely new drug discovery.

Now, at the same time, the company is leaning a lot more into convenience-driven and differentiated formulations. They have that low-dose estrogen weekly contraceptive patch, Xulane Lo, which has already shown quite favorable efficacy/safety. It also has the potential for a best-in-class profile in its category, so that could be one aspect to help defend pricing and margins against the lower cost competition. I also see the additional near-term launches as key to helping improve the portfolio mix by 2023. We can look at pitolisant for sleep disorders and sotagliflozin for cardiometabolic conditions, as well as expected continued growth from some of their durable legacy brands like Lipitor, Norvasc, and EpiPen. These still generate rather meaningful global revenue streams. So I think this strategy is less about any single blockbuster and more about incremental, diversified growth. With the aim of spreading that growth across dozens of products to reduce risk/dependency on any single one. The differentiation and benefit should come from things like lifecycle management, geographic expansion, and slight formulation improvements as a collection, driving steady revenue expansion. The bonus here is that Viatris also has the operating leverage to pair with it. The review found an expected $650 million in gross cost savings, so even with all the portfolio mix changes, this should also directly support EBITDA expansion, even if we see revenue growth remain modest simply due to how competitive the market could become by 2030.

Second, we have some near-term product launches that should serve as a key incremental driver layered on top of that base case I just outlined. Management here mentions launches like fast-acting meloxicam, pitolisant, and Effexor for GAD in Japan. I know I mentioned these in the base case; however, I see them as kind of in between as a middle layer of growth here. With the product launches kind of bridging that gap between the stable base business and the more uncertain long-term upside from pipeline assets and M&A. So I like to view these as examples of lower-risk, commercially visible opportunities that could realistically begin contributing revenue within the next 1 to 3 years. I don’t see these as transformational blockbusters, but they should incrementally help build revenue and product mix. So the step up from the base case, a 3% to 4% revenue CAGR, toward the broader 5% to 6% target could definitely come from these areas. Just to add on to that, the way I see it is these launches are also somewhat in the middle because they combine a relatively high probability of success with a meaningful but not massive outsized impact. If we recall, management has guided to $450 to $550 million in new product revenues in 2026 alone, up from about $324 million in 2025, which goes to show how this layer is already scaling.

The third engine here would be the innovative side of the pipeline. I see upside here, particularly with some of their potential blockbuster assets like selatogrel and cenerimod. The expectation is for these alone to add around +1% to long-term revenue growth if successful. And finally, which is pretty critical for the higher-end targets, management is relying heavily on business development funded by strong cash flow. The story here is for over $11 billion in expected deployable cash through 2030, with about half of that sum earmarked for acquisitions or partnerships. The latter here could potentially add another +1% revenue growth and a larger +3% uplift to EBITDA and EPS growth.

Valuation

Viatris currently trades at about $13.50, with a market cap of $15.82 billion. The enterprise value is a lot higher at $29.01 billion. Now, even with the solid revenue base, the market still seems to be pricing the stock at a big discount compared to the peer group. The trailing non-GAAP price to earnings ratio sits at about 5.82x versus the Health Care sector median of 16.81x. Which is nearly a 65% discount. Elsewhere, the trailing EV to EBITDA sits at 7.33x, again at a discount, about 46% below the sector median. So there’s clearly some investor caution here around growth prospects or execution risk, even with the strong cash flow generation. Keep in mind that cash flow is projected to exceed $2.7 billion annually by 2030 under those new base case targets.

Elsewhere, price-to-sales and price-to-book ratios again show this discount. They’re trading at 1.12x and 1.08x, respectively, well below sector medians, which would suggest that the market is valuing Viatris a bit more conservatively, likely after factoring in all the uncertainties around near-term launches as well as pipeline development. There is also the debt leverage to consider here. So overall, I think the current valuation shows that investors are focusing a lot more on potential risks rather than the long-term financial growth management just outlined, hence the slow sell-off in the stock since earnings.

Risks and looking ahead

So, put simply, I don’t think management is assuming organic growth alone will get them to that 5% to 6% revenue CAGR. The base business plus the launches get them to 3% to 4%, whilst quite a lot is dependent on pipeline success and M&A. These are particularly important to help bridge the gap to the full target. This is also an important point for valuation, because it essentially means a meaningful portion of the plan depends on execution and external deal-making, not just the existing assets.

Now the risk here is in that middle layer. You see, a lot depends on those launches, and whilst they’re lower risk than early-stage pipeline assets, they’re not actually guaranteed contributors at scale. Commercial uptake here could be a bit slower than expected due to pricing pressure or competition from generics and alternative therapies, particularly in those cost-sensitive markets. Of course, there is also execution risk around the launches and regulatory approvals, especially for products like pitolisant and new indications of Effexor in markets like Japan. But since each product is relatively small individually, the overall strategy relies on consistent execution across those multiple launches, so the risk is spread out here. However, underperformance in a few can materially impact the overall growth bridge toward the 5% to 6% revenue target the market now expects.

data.title: Arcos Dorados: Q4 Earnings Don't Merit An Upgrade
data.description:
Arcos Dorados (NYSE:ARCO) is the primary Latin American and Caribbean franchising partner for fast food giant McDonald's (MCD). The name Arcos Dorados means "Golden Arches" in Spanish, and Arcos Dorados has become a large and valuable partner for the corporate parent.

However, while Arcos Dorados generates plenty of revenues, it has struggled to turn that into durable value creation for its outside shareholders. Shares remain far below their IPO price from about 15 years ago:

I last wrote about Arcos Dorados in June of 2025, arguing that the company didn't have such a golden outlook.

That's played out largely as expected, with ARCO stock going nowhere over the past year. That leaves it far behind both the benchmark regional ETF and direct LatAm fast food competitor Alsea (ALSSF) over the past 12 months:

Has this continued underperformance created a buying opportunity where ARCO could close the relative valuation gap against other LatAm stocks? Unfortunately, I don't think so. Let's take a look at recent developments for Arcos Dorados.

Q4 Earnings & Broader Business Outlook

Arcos Dorados reported its Q4 and full-year 2025 results this week. Q4 saw earnings of 13 cents, which missed expectations by seven cents and fell from the 28 cents reported in the same quarter of the prior year. Revenues of $1.27 billion grew 11% year-over-year and came in slightly ahead of expectations.

While Q4 wasn't particularly strong, for the full-year, Arcos Dorados reported EPS of $1.01, which was up sharply from the 71 cents reported in 2024. Good news, right?

There is a big caveat to Arcos Dorado's 2025 earnings, however. Here it the key item, as quoted from the Q4 press release:

"Results included a net tax benefit in Brazil of $33.8 million in the fourth quarter and $159.0 million for the full year, which is expected to convert to cash over the next five years."

Arcos Dorados generated $212 million in total net income for full-year 2025, so the tax item ended up representing a large chunk of the firm's overall profitability. As of this writing, analysts are modeling a 37% decline in the company's FY'26 earnings per share as the company is unlikely to enjoy a similar tax benefit once again next year. And, given that Q4 wasn't particularly strong in isolation, don't be surprised if analysts trim the 2026 EPS number a bit in coming days and weeks.

Before moving on, I want to stop and comment on revenues for a minute. Arcos Dorados has reported absolutely massive revenue growth over the past five years, with the headline figure doubling since 2021. It might seem like the business is sizzling right now.

Not so fast.

Here's a chart of the company's revenues, reported in USD, since the IPO:

While revenues have indeed skyrocketed recently, they are up less than 20% versus the prior early 2010s peak. And there was a huge decline in the interim period. The overall result is a poor long-term CAGR since IPO, especially for a business that is still opening new restaurant locations on net.

So, what's going on here?

The issue is that Arcos Dorados measures same store sales in local currency, and those local currencies often tend to devalue against the U.S. Dollar. Argentina is a special case, as that country recently had a hyperinflationary spell; the Argentine Peso lost 99% of its value against the U.S. Dollar between 2015 and 2025. And, even putting Argentina aside, Brazil has run double-digit inflation rates in various years recently, and several other Latin American countries have faced currency devaluation as well.

However, over the past two years, we've seen a sell-off in the value of the U.S. Dollar and a strong appreciation in the value of various Latin American currencies amid the stronger commodity price environment. This has given Arcos Dorados a big push in terms of its reported revenues when displayed in USD. However, this doesn't necessarily translate to earnings growth, as the company's labor and other expenses are largely in local currency as well and those grow in proportion to local revenues. Fellow Seeking Alpha author Quipus Capital wrote a good piece last year giving further detail on how ARCO is reporting strong sales growth even as the underlying business may be deteriorating.

Potential Positive Developments

I don't want to be overly negative here. While I'm not bullish on ARCO stock, in the interest of fairness, it's worth highlighting some positive points.

First, there are potential positive political changes in the air. Two of Arcos Dorados' key markets are Brazil and Argentina, respectively. Brazil has a presidential election this fall, and betting markets assign roughly 50/50 odds that the current socialist government will give way to a more market-friendly successor. This could lift valuations for Brazilian stocks immediately and promote a stronger consumer spending environment over the next few years.

And, in Argentina, hyper-capitalist president Javier Milei's political party performed much better than expected in Fall 2025 legislative elections. This gave Milei's party a fresh mandate to continue its sweeping pro-business reforms. Arcos Dorados has struggled in Argentina under a difficult regulatory regime, high labor costs, etc. If Milei can continue to transition Argentina toward a more business-friendly approach, it's possible that Arcos Dorados could start to generate sustainably higher profit margins from Argentina going forward.

And there's also the wild card that is Venezuela. The U.S. engaged in a military operation to remove long-time leader Nicolas Maduro earlier this year. It remains to be seen what exactly is going to happen with the new Venezuelan government. However, Arcos Dorados continues to operate dozens of restaurants in Venezuela and it's plausible that the company could be able to open a lot more stores and see strong sales growth if the Venezuelan economy returns to more normalized operating conditions.

There is also a potential organic growth story. Arcos Dorados opened 102 new restaurants in 2025, which was slightly above guidance and outpaced the 85 new store figure from the prior year. While I'm not convinced McDonald's is all that strong of a brand in Latin America, a rising economic tide should lift all boats and allow the company to grow at a faster clip regardless.

And on that last point, it's possible that the current macroeconomic environment will continue to benefit LatAm and thus provide a boost for all its consumer stocks. We saw a huge run in the prices of metals such as gold, silver and copper over the past 12 months. And now the price of oil is surging, which helps oil exporters such as Venezuela, Colombia, and Argentina. There's also talk of potential food shortages and price squeezes in grains due to the Strait of Hormuz issue, which could benefit food exporters like Brazil and Argentina. This could trickle down to the median LatAm consumer and help spark increased consumption in the restaurant sector.

ARCO Bottom Line

As my disclosure shows, I own shares in Alsea and I do not have a position in Arcos Dorados. Even after 50% outperformance from Alsea versus Arcos Dorados over the past year, I am sticking with that view. I expect greater upside for Alsea from today's prices than Arcos Dorados.

Fundamentally, I just don't view McDonald's as being a particularly well-positioned brand in Latin America. It's not cheap enough to be a real budget option in many markets around here. But I don't see the McDonald's brand, in the year 2026, as having enough luxury cache to drive a pricing premium versus local options, either. I live in a fairly small Colombian city. Even here, far removed from major tourist hotspots, we now have craft hamburgers on par with what you might find in the United States. Meanwhile, the quality of budget hamburgers has also gone up, increasing competition with McDonald's across the price/quality spectrum.

Of course, there's still a place in the Latin American market for McDonald's. The existing restaurants are running profitably, and the company is opening a reasonable number of new locations. But this is structurally a low-margin business, and margins are once again heading in the wrong direction.

Compare Arcos Dorados versus Alsea and the difference is rather stark:

ARCO stock is trading at about 12x forward earnings, which seems like the right sort of multiple for this sort of low-growth low-margin business that has a high level of economic exposure to volatile markets such as Brazil, Argentina, and Venezuela. The company can put together a good year or two from time to time when local macroeconomics are conducive. But it's hard to generate much long-term shareholder value starting from a weak growth profile, while running on a median 5% operating margin in often unstable economies.

ARCO stock is cheap enough that there could be upside if good things happen. An election win for the conservatives in Brazil combined with a real reopening of the Venezuelan economy, for example, would be a nice set-up for a stronger 2027 at Arcos Dorados, should those events occur. But, over the longer-term, I don't have much confidence that ARCO stock will outperform other LatAm consumer stocks or the iShares Latin America 40 ETF (ILF) more broadly. As such, I'm sticking with a hold rating.
